surgery. With respect to the upcoming Romania mission, he informed HSD that he had consulted
with an orthopedic surgeon who would provide him with a semi-open cast for his flights (to be
changed again to a closed cast once he was back in Bishkek). The Applicant stated that the
Romania country office was arranging for a wheelchair for him and preparing a mission schedule
that minimized movement between offices. He also requested business class authorization for his
flight to Romania.
20. The next day, on 10 September 2018, a Senior Occupational Health Specialist in HSD
advised the Applicant to go on sick leave or STD, stating:
My advice is that you should avail yourself of sick leave and short term disability,
until such time you are able to mobilize with minimal assistance and you[r]
physicians are in agreement with the amount of travel and activity that you have
proposed is in accordance with your post-operative recovery.
I would suggest that you reach out to Broadspire [the World Bank Group’s
Workers’ Compensation Claims Administrator (Claims Administrator)] to start an
STD claim.
21. That same day, the Applicant responded to the Senior Occupational Health Specialist
asking the following:
Many thanks for the clear reply—one more question. In case the doctor
recommends not to go on [the] business trip to Romania, and I decide to go anyway
in order to accommodate the request of management, would this affect my
insurance coverage in case of medical complications while on business trip?
22. Later that day, the Senior Occupational Health Specialist responded by providing a contact
from the compensation and benefits team. He added, “I would take as much time as is medically
necessary so as not to compromise your recent surgery and recovery.”
23. On 13 September 2018, the Applicant emailed Mr. A an update, stating in part:

6
Sept 14 (Netherlands) – check up and change of cast. I have asked the hospital to
change the closed cast to an open cast (pre-condition for flying), if specialist allows
me to fly, which I assume s/he will. […] [S]ee you next week in Romania.
(Emphasis in original.)
24. Thereafter, the Applicant traveled to Romania. He states that during the Romania mission
he faced considerable difficulties, and his health situation worsened.
25. On 21 September 2018, the Applicant returned to Bishkek.
26. On 25 September 2018, the Applicant went to get his cast changed and, according to him,
that is when his doctors in Bishkek discovered that the surgical wound had become infected.
27. On 1 October 2018, the Applicant emailed Mr. A to inform him that there were “some
complications” with his recovery after returning from Romania, that he had submitted a sick leave
request, and that he was processing a claim for STD given his medical conditions. Mr. A responded
the same day, stating, “Sorry to hear that. Indeed recovery takes full priority.”
28. On 8 October 2018, the Applicant commenced STD, which was eventually extended
several times until 9 October 2020, at which point the Applicant was medically cleared to work.
29. On 15 October 2018, the Applicant underwent a second surgery in the Netherlands that
entailed “debriding the infection.”
30. On 17 October 2018, the Applicant and Mr. A exchanged emails regarding the logistics of
the Applicant’s leave. The Applicant wrote, in part, “I’ll be honest with you re: Romania-travel. It
was a bad idea to go there, and I really regret doing that. I had a significant setback to my recovery,
and 100% recovery is not an option anymore now.” Mr. A replied the same day, stating, “P S noted
and lesson learned for both of us on Romania. Health and family should always come before
business, and maybe we forgot that. I hope the recovery will be OK still.”

7
31. On 13 December 2018, in an email discussion between the Applicant, Mr. A, and another
colleague, Mr. A stated, “Both: thanks for this. For now we can ‘hide’ behind the lack of [a] more
committed [government] request, but once the team feels ready (and [the Applicant]—let’s not
repeat the experience of the last mission of course) I think fielding a follow-up mission will anyway
help unlock things.”
32. On 3 March 2019, the Applicant wrote to Mr. A regarding his health and continued medical
evacuation status, stating in part:
Once this whole episode is behind us, I would appreciate a conversation with
management [about] how we ended up in this situation. Flying on this September
[2018] mission to Romania and onwards to Bishkek really impacted my post-
surgery recovery.
33. That same day, Mr. A responded to the Applicant’s email, stating:
Indeed, with [hindsight] the decision to have you lead the mission last fall was a
mistake and I think we all misjudged the situation back then—I’ll be the first one
to admit (including your doctor if I recall). That certainly will serve as a lesson
learn[ed] for me and from now on, I would rather err on the side of caution and
have someone else take some of the operational workload than take a risk of having
you back any time too soon.
34. On 15 March 2019, the Applicant underwent a third surgery, again for debriding and
closing the wound.
35. On 26 March 2019, Mr. A obtained an exceptional clearance for the Applicant to maintain
his operational posting in Bishkek to complete his medical evacuation in the Netherlands. On the
same day, the Applicant wrote to Mr. A, stating, “Many thanks—I know how much of a hassle this
was to arrange.”
36. On 23 July 2019, the Applicant and Mr. A discussed the Applicant’s annual Overall
Performance Evaluation (OPE) and his potential work program upon remapping back to the Global
Unit of the Water GP. Following this discussion, Mr. A reached out to three Practice Managers in

8
the Africa Region on behalf of the Applicant to inquire about potential work opportunities and
urged the Applicant to reach out to these managers directly.
37. On 2 September 2019, Mr. A emailed the Applicant, stating:
[I]t would be good if you initiate discussions with the Africa PMs [Practice
Managers] soon, so they have a bit of time to think about it and when you’re back
you can get a sense of work program. [The then Global Director of the Water GP
(Water Global Director)] also mentioned the importance of reaching out to [a
Practice Manager in the Water GP (Practice Manager)] as the head of the GP’s
central services unit—and to herself to the extent you see fit of course.
38. On 4 September 2019, the Applicant replied, stating, “Yes, will reach out to GP
management—will do so after some leave, need to put this annus horribilis behind me.”
39. On 5 September 2019, Mr. A responded to the Applicant, stating, “I am actually surprised
that you have not reached out to the GP management yet, despite us discussing this in July
already—this is more urgent than you seem to think,” and again urged the Applicant to do so.
40. On the same day, the Applicant responded to Mr. A, stating in part:
I might be a bit over-sensitive to this, but after Sept 2018, when I clearly wrote to
you that I did not feel ready to go on mission to Romania so short after my
operation, and then felt pressured by you during our WebEx conversation to go on
mission anyway, because you made it clear that [I] needed to redeem myself to
management and show my performance, I paid a high price for not listening to my
body. It was irresponsible doing a WB mission with an open surgery wound on [my]
leg—the rest is history and I am still recovering. For my recovery now I only
consult with my treating physician and the Medical Team at the Bank. I realize that
the optics of this with you and broader GP management, are not good, but my
physician has my best interests in mind and I listen to him firstly, communicated
that to [the Claims Administrator] and the WB health team, and follow their
instructions.
41. On 10 September 2019, upon exchange of the draft comments on the Applicant’s OPE, the
Applicant sent an email to Mr. A, stating:

9
I am fully aware that there is a strong perception with the practice that I am not
keen on coming back to DC and engaging with the practice. We discussed this
already at the end of FY2018 that was fully agreed and understood, and I am
committed to coming back, in Bishkek at this moment to arrange this. I know you
are one of my strongest advocates in the GP management team (which I
acknowledge and very much appreciate) and that you went out of your way [at the]
end [of] July to alert the AFR [Africa Region] GP team that I would reach out to
them. Apologies for not picking that up immediately, but a couple of days after our
July conversation I found myself at a family funeral, and a couple of days later at
the treating physician, who again noted that my treatment would be longer than
thought.
42. On 1 November 2019, the Applicant was remapped to the Global Unit in the Water GP
(SWAGL), reporting to the Practice Manager. As of that date, the Applicant also returned to full
STD and remained on full STD through October 2020. Although the new position’s duty station
was Washington, D.C., the Applicant states that he initially remained in Bishkek because the
COVID-19 pandemic delayed his relocation to Washington, D.C., and that he later temporarily
relocated to Novosibirsk, Russian Federation (Russia), due to his wife’s medical needs.
43. On 8 June 2020, the Applicant submitted his initial claim for workers’ compensation
benefits to the Claims Administrator.
44. On 26 June 2020, the Claims Administrator issued a determination denying the Applicant’s
claim as untimely.
45. On 25 September 2020, following discussions regarding the denial of the Applicant’s
workers’ compensation claim, the Applicant provided additional medical records and explanations
to the Claims Administrator.
46. On 9 October 2020, the Applicant’s STD period ended, and he resumed full-time remote
work.
47. On 15 October 2020—as part of the Bank’s internal reorganization—the Applicant was
offered a Mutually Agreed Separation (MAS) or Early-Out option, which had been extended to

10
other staff members in the Water GP as part of the realignment exercise. The Bank states that,
while other staff members accepted the MAS, the Applicant did not.
48. According to the Bank, due to home-based work resulting from the COVID-19 pandemic,
the Applicant continued telecommuting from various locations, including the Kyrgyz Republic,
the Netherlands, and Russia, for over a year. During this period, the Applicant was mapped to the
Global Unit of the Water GP in Washington, D.C. and was assigned by Water GP management to
provide cross-unit support to the African Region and to conduct ad hoc assignments for the Global
Unit.
49. On 30 January 2021, the Water GP launched its FY21 batch rotation exercise. The
announcement specifically stated that “[a]pplications are due in Compass [the World Bank’s job
application portal] by February 12.”
50. On 13 February 2021, the Water Global Director discovered that the Applicant had missed
the application deadline. She then emailed the Applicant, stating, “Please send your applications
now by email and we will ask HR to load them into the system.” Ultimately, the Applicant was
shortlisted and interviewed for two positions in Jakarta and Lilongwe but was not selected for
either.
51. According to the Applicant, on 26 May 2021, he met via Webex with the Water Global
Director and an HR Manager. The Applicant states that, during the meeting, the Water Global
Director notified him for the first time that his position in SWAGL was temporary. According to
the Applicant, the Water Global Director emphasized that due to budgetary issues the Applicant
would likely be separated from the Bank within twelve months if he was unable to secure another
position.
52. The Applicant states that, following his 26 May 2021 meeting with the Water Global
Director and the HR Manager, the Applicant met with the Practice Manager via Webex on 9 June
2021 to further discuss his job situation. The Applicant states that the Practice Manager reiterated
the message conveyed by the Water Global Director—that his SWAGL position had always been

11
only an interim arrangement and that he would need to quickly secure a position outside of the
Global Unit.
53. On 26 July 2021, the Practice Manager sent an email to the Applicant, copying the Water
Global Director and the HR Manager, confirming the contents of their June 2021 conversation,
including the temporary nature of his SWAGL position and the possibility of separation from the
Bank if he was unable to secure another position. The email stated in part:
It was good to talk with you on June [9], 2021 to follow up on the conversation that
you had with [the Water Global Director] and [the HR Manager] on your career
plan and the outcome of the FY21 Batch Recruitment. I would like to once again
confirm, that given the restructuring of the Bank during the past two years, which
shifted the Global Practice staff to the regions while maintaining only a small team
in the Global Unit, there is no longer a work program to support the position to
which you were assigned (on a temporary basis) as you transitioned from your
posting in ECA [Europe and Central Asia] during your STD.
We have also noted that you have followed our advice and applied to a number of
positions during the past Water GP cluster recruitment processes, however, have
not been selected. As such, we strongly encourage you to place priority attention in
this FY to finding a new assignment within the World Bank Group or externally. If
you are unable to find a position in the Water GP or in other parts in the Bank by
the end of this FY (June 30, 2022), we will discuss with you options for separation
from Bank employment. These options could come into effect at any time by June
30, 2022.
54. On 7 December 2021, the Water GP launched its annual batch rotation exercise for FY22.
The communication to staff included links to the new mobility framework and the water
competencies framework. The email stated the following:
All IRS [Internationally Recruited Staff] who will have completed 4 years in their
current assignment by July 2022, as informed by their PM, are required to
participate in the process. Other GP staff seeking a promotion or conversion to IRS,
as well as those who have been at least 3 years in their current assignment (for
same-grade application), are also welcome to apply. […]
Applications are due in Compass by December 17.
55. On 14 December 2021, the Applicant emailed the Water Global Director, copying the
Practice Manager, requesting a meeting to receive feedback and guidance on the batch rotation

12
exercise. The Applicant also stated his intention to apply for three positions in Bamako,
“Beijing/HQ [headquarters],” and Yaoundé.
56. On 17 December 2021, the Water Global Director responded to the Applicant, stating:
Thank you for reaching out. As the hiring managers are in charge of the process, I
would suggest you contact them directly in case you need information on the job
listings. You may also want to seek feedback from the chair of the panel from the
positions for which you interviewed.

Based on the above investigation, the First Tier Reviewer was unable to establish

information about the Requester’s mental health condition was shared by [the

uncover any processing of personal data that failed to meet the standards set out in
Principle 1 of the Privacy Policy’s requirements for legitimate, fair and transparent
processing.
Determination
Based on the foregoing, the Call for Review does not articulate a cognizable
violation of Principles 1–6 or 7(b)(i) of the Privacy Policy.

Furthermore, in view of the Applicant’s clarification that he was not challenging the temporary

24
114. On 16 November 2024, the Applicant separated from the Bank.
SUMMARY OF THE CONTENTIONS OF THE PARTIES
The Applicant’s Contention No. 1
The redundancy decision constituted an egregious abuse of managerial discretion
115. The Applicant contends that the redundancy decision constituted an egregious abuse of
managerial discretion. The Applicant contends that management violated his due process rights
with respect to the redundancy decision in at least five ways.
The obligation of transparency
116. First, the Applicant contends that management breached its obligation of transparency by
failing to timely alert the Applicant that his SWAGL position was slated for redundancy and that
holding this position would hinder his ability to secure a new position through the batch rotation
process.
117. The Applicant contends that he was reassigned to SWAGL on 1 November 2019, while he
was on STD, without any prior consultation or explanation from management. The Applicant
contends that Water GP management did not initially disclose any of the detrimental effects of the
remapping and only later, in May 2021, informed him that his SWAGL position had always been
“temporary” and slated for redundancy as part of the ongoing shift of staff and resources from
headquarters to the regions. The Applicant submits that it was not until February 2022 that he
learned that the “temporary” nature of his SWAGL position also excluded him from the generally
applicable mobility exercise rules designed to facilitate staff rotation rather than to separate staff
members from employment.
118. The Applicant asserts that management imposed this remapping notwithstanding (i) Mr.
A’s prior assurances that the Applicant would remain mapped to ECA, based at headquarters,
following the conclusion of his field assignment; and (ii) the Applicant’s guaranteed reentry to a

25
“suitable position,” as stated in the Memorandum of Extended Assignment to Bishkek. The
Applicant further asserts that his remapping occurred in contravention of Staff Rule 6.22,
paragraph 9.01, which he states “obligates the Bank to ‘seek[] to facilitate the Staff Member’s
return to work prior to the completion of 24 months of STD, when […] the Staff Member is able
to perform the material duties of their regular job.’” The Applicant further contends, “Not only
that, but management expressly acknowledged that [he] was remapped into a temporary position
because he was on STD.”
119. To the Applicant, each of these facts—the temporary nature of the SWAGL position and
the effect of this status on his participation in future mobility exercises—had a material impact on
his career and on the eventual redundancy decision. Thus, the Applicant contends, fundamental
principles of transparency required management to alert him to these “career-affecting facts ‘with
all possible anticipation’—i.e., when they became known to management.”
The obligation to strictly follow the pertinent rules relating to the redundancy process
120. The Applicant contends that management failed to follow at least six sets of rules or
policies that could have potentially helped the Applicant avoid redundancy.
121. First, the Applicant notes that the Bank owes a duty of care to all staff members to “provide
a safe and healthy work environment to its staff.” However, in the present case, the Applicant
contends that Mr. A breached this duty in multiple ways, including by (i) pressuring the Applicant
to travel to Romania over his clearly expressed hesitations immediately after surgery, (ii) failing
to “make the necessary inquiries” into whether the Applicant’s condition could be reasonably
accommodated on mission and instead leaving it to the Applicant to try and sort out his own
medical accommodations, and (iii) continuing to push the Applicant to attend mission meetings
even after it became perfectly clear that the Applicant’s need for rest and elevation of his leg could
not be accommodated.
122. The Applicant further contends that Mr. A’s actions not only breached the Bank’s duty of
care but were also directly at odds with the core purposes of the Bank’s disability programs and

26
the clear guidance that managers like Mr. A, as well as HR Business Partners (HRBPs), receive on
communicating and effectuating those goals. The Applicant submits that it was Mr. A’s actions and
the serious, long-lasting, and foreseeable medical complications that set in motion the multiyear
series of events that eventually led to the Applicant being set up for redundancy.
123. Second, the Applicant avers that Water GP management failed to meaningfully honor the
Applicant’s reentry guarantee, which provided for his return to a “suitable position” following his
Bishkek field assignment. To the Applicant, whatever else “suitable” might mean, it cannot fairly
be read to contemplate returning a staff member on an open-ended appointment to a position that
had already been slated for redundancy. The Applicant further asserts, “Certainly a reentry
guarantee does not shield a re-entering staff member from future business-related changes
indefinitely, but if a position can be slated for elimination before a re-entering staff member is
placed into it, then a reentry guarantee would be hardly worth the paper it is printed on.” With
regard to the Bank’s contention that the Applicant nullified his reentry guarantee upon requesting
extensions to it, the Applicant contends that the original extension request in September 2018 was
suggested by the HR Operations Team due to his health condition and the additional extensions
were necessitated by his ongoing medical treatment and STD leave.
124. Third, the Applicant submits that Water GP management seems to have gone outside of the
Staff Rules in deeming his SWAGL position to be “temporary” given that nothing in the Staff
Rules appears to contemplate or authorize such a status—a status that contravenes the mandate of
Staff Rule 6.22, paragraph 9.01, to return a staff member on STD to his or her “regular job.”
According to the Applicant, to date he has yet to see management identify any rules, guidelines,
or other applicable authority for deeming his position temporary.
125. Fourth, the Applicant contends that Water GP management failed to treat him fairly under
the applicable telecommuting rules by refusing to approve his ending his telecommute and
returning to his assigned duty station in Washington, D.C. for nearly a year. The Applicant contends
that there is nothing in the applicable rules that suggests that a manager can deny a staff member’s
request to report to his assigned duty station, yet the former Water Global Director repeatedly
refused to approve his requests to end his telecommute and report to his duty station, and

27
repeatedly refused to sign off on his relocation travel until after the Russian invasion of Ukraine,
only months ahead of his redundancy. To the Applicant, being in Washington, D.C. earlier could
have helped him network with hiring managers or learn of projects or portfolios in other regions
that may have better positioned him for vacancies. However, the Applicant contends that, by the
time the former Water Global Director approved his relocation travel to Washington, D.C. in March
2022, it was after he had been declared redundant and excluded from the FY22 batch rotation
exercise, and this was “simply too little, too late.”
126. Fifth, the Applicant contends that Water GP management failed to adhere to the rules it
circulated to all Water GP staff, including the Applicant, regarding the FY22 batch rotation exercise
process by failing to shortlist him for at least one position. The Applicant notes the PRS Panel’s
finding that management’s announcement of the batch rotation exercise “d[id] not make any
distinction between staff on permanent or temporary positions as a condition to participate in the
batch recruitment, or for inclusion in at least one shortlist,” and thus, “[the Applicant], as a staff
member in good standing, had every right to be part of the batch.” To the Applicant, the PRS
Panel’s only error on this issue was in failing to appreciate the consequence of management’s
violation of the rules, namely that, but for management’s breach, the Applicant might have
succeeded in securing a new position through the FY22 process and thus avoiding the redundancy
notice that followed shortly thereafter.
127. Sixth, the Applicant contends that, as already established by PRS, Water GP management
failed to adhere to the rules relating to the FY22 batch rotation exercise in a second way—by
failing to fairly evaluate him against the advertised selection criteria for the Yaoundé position. The
Applicant notes that the PRS Panel found that, despite Bank rules requiring hiring decisions to
follow the vacancy notice, management declined to shortlist him for the Yaoundé position based
on his alleged insufficiency in FCV experience and French proficiency—criteria not listed in the
Terms of Reference (TOR). The Applicant avers that, even if these criteria had been properly
advertised, the decision to not shortlist him on these bases would still constitute an abuse of
discretion given his demonstrated record of experience over many years of leading high-profile
Bank projects in the West Bank and Gaza and his demonstrated proficiency in French as formally
recognized by the Bank.

28
The redundancy decision was improperly motivated
128. The Applicant contends that management’s actions leading up to the redundancy decision
were improperly motivated by the Applicant’s disability status and/or by retaliatory animus after
the Applicant suggested that Mr. A acted improperly in pressuring him to travel to Romania.
129. With respect to the Applicant’s medical condition and disability status, the Applicant
contends that, prior to the Romania mission in September 2018, there was a clear, agreed-upon
understanding that the Applicant would finish his Bishkek assignment, relocate to Washington,
D.C. while remaining mapped to ECA, and spend necessary time building relationships and
reintegrating with the GP before moving on to another region. However, according to the
Applicant, it was only after it became clear that his medical condition and resulting disability leave
would continue well into 2019 that the situation abruptly changed. The Applicant asserts that this
was when, “out of the blue,” management informed him—without any contemporaneous
explanation of why—that he was being remapped to SWAGL.
130. To the Applicant, the only explanation that seems to fit is that
Mr. [A] wanted [the Applicant] out of his unit once it became clear that his medical
issues and disability leave would be ongoing, and [the former Water Global
Director] saw the mandate to shift resources out of the GP and into the regions as
an opportunity to remove [the Applicant] from ECA—because of his disability
status—without rotating him into another region. (Emphasis in original.)
131. With respect to the issue of retaliation, the Applicant asserts that he believed that Mr. A’s
actions in pressuring him to travel to Romania in September 2018 breached applicable Bank
policies and thus constituted “suspected misconduct” within the meaning of Staff Rule 3.00. The
Applicant further asserts that he raised these concerns directly with Mr. A, then his line manager,
on at least two occasions—once on 3 March 2019 and again on 5 September 2019. Notably, in the
second instance, the Applicant avers that Mr. A brought the former Water Global Director into the
conversation, making her aware of his allegations.

29
132. From that point, according to the Applicant, everything changed. Prior to this point, the
Applicant contends that he had more than a decade of strong performance reviews, a documented
history of successful project leadership as a Task Team Leader, and a full work program throughout
ECA and beyond, including in the West Bank and Gaza. Almost immediately afterward, however,
the Applicant contends that he was shunted into what turned out to be a dead-end position in
SWAGL, a fact that he avers the former Water Global Director was undoubtedly aware of at the
time she directed his remapping. Furthermore, the Applicant avers that he was repeatedly
instructed to stay on his telecommute and not return to Washington, D.C. and was excluded from
participating in batch rotation exercises for two years in a row.
133. In sum, the Applicant contends that the entire chain of events from September 2019 onward
appears to have been calculated to usher him out of the Bank after he made clear that he believed
he had been mistreated with respect to the Romania mission and that he intended to continue
pressing this issue.
The lack of genuine effort to find the Applicant an alternative position
134. The Applicant contends that management breached its obligation to make a genuine effort
to find the Applicant an alternative position for which he was qualified and that would allow him
to avoid redundancy.
135. The Applicant asserts that this obligation arose once Water GP management determined
that his SWAGL position was to be eliminated. To the Applicant, there is no evidence that the
former Water Global Director, the Practice Manager, or anyone else ever made such an effort.
Additionally, the Applicant asserts that management on multiple occasions deliberately withheld
such assistance, even when he specifically sought it out.
136. The Applicant contends, for example, that, after management’s announcement of the FY22
batch rotation exercise, he reached out to both the former Water Global Director and the Practice
Manager for feedback and guidance on which positions he should apply for. He asserts that neither
of them provided any meaningful substantive advice. The Applicant further contends that, during

30
the FY22 batch rotation exercise, management chose to deliberately exclude him from this process
despite, as the PRS Panel found, there being nothing in the Water GP’s batch rotation exercise
guidelines to suggest that he was ineligible. In sum, the Applicant submits that management
affirmatively denied him the types of support given to all other Water GP staff who needed to
rotate, which falls far short of what management’s obligation of genuine assistance requires.
Career mismanagement
137. The Applicant contends that the redundancy decision was the flawed end result of multiple
managerial “errors of judgment” that, taken together, amount to the unreasonable and arbitrary
mismanagement of the Applicant’s career.
138. In sum, the Applicant contends that the impugned redundancy decision was the flawed
product of multiple improper actions by Water GP management. To the Applicant, regardless of
whatever discretion the Bank might have to shift resources and positions from headquarters to the
regions, the Applicant’s job never should have been put at risk by that process but for
management’s repeated efforts to use the process as an opportunity to get rid of him in violation
of numerous rules, policies, and managerial obligations. For these reasons, the Applicant contends
that the redundancy decision constitutes an egregious abuse of managerial discretion and should
be overturned.
The Bank’s Response
The redundancy decision was based on legitimate business needs, and there was no abuse of
discretion
139. The Bank contends that the Applicant has not established a prima facie case of abuse of
discretion in any managerial decisions. Specifically, the Bank submits that the redundancy decision
was made on a reasonable and observable basis, that it was grounded in legitimate business needs,
and that all applicable procedures were strictly observed in the process. The Bank further submits
that the Applicant has not met the threshold established by the Tribunal to suggest that management
abused its power when making the redundancy decision.

31
140. With respect to the Applicant’s claims that the redundancy decision was improperly
motivated by either discrimination based on his disability status or retaliatory animus after he
suggested that Mr. A acted improperly in pressuring him to travel to Romania, the Bank contends
that the Applicant has failed to present a prima facie case of retaliation. To the Bank, the
Applicant’s allegations not only are speculative but also lack any factual support in the record. The
Bank asserts that there is no credible evidence linking the managerial decision to any
discriminatory or retaliatory motive, and the record clearly demonstrates that these decisions were
based on legitimate operational and business needs rather than any improper considerations.
141. Further, the Bank asserts that the Applicant appears to suggest the existence of an elaborate
conspiracy against him, allegedly involving a Senior Managing Director, a Vice President, multiple
Global Directors, several managers, at least three HRBPs, and numerous other Bank colleagues—
all purportedly conspiring to remove him from the institution, solely because of his extended STDs
and sick leave. The Bank asserts that these allegations are entirely unfounded.
142. Moreover, the Bank contends that the Applicant’s assertions entirely overlook the
significant efforts made by management to support him. The Bank contends that, despite the
Applicant’s persistent resistance and refusal to follow guidance on multiple fronts, including
delaying his relocation to Washington, D.C., Water GP management exercised extraordinary
patience and provided extensive accommodations. The Bank contends that the record shows that
both the former Water Global Director and Mr. A actively supported the Applicant by facilitating
late application submissions, coordinating medical support and benefits, offering career guidance,
and even securing exceptional clearance for his medical evacuation. The Bank asserts that these
actions directly contradict the Applicant’s narrative of pretext, ill motive, or a coordinated
managerial effort to exclude him from available positions within the Water GP, leading to his
eventual departure.
143. Next, the Bank contends that the redundancy decision had a reasonable and observable
basis.

32
144. The Bank submits that the redundancy decision was made within the broader context of
the Bank’s workforce planning exercise which began in 2019 and which sought to promote
efficient administration by enhancing the Bank’s capacity to achieve favorable outcomes for
member countries and to strengthen its development impact and expertise on global issues.
According to the Bank, as part of the July 2020 Global Practices realignment, technical positions
and their budgets within the Water GP were shifted from headquarters to regional offices, placing
responsibility for these roles with the regions. The Bank states that this restructuring led to major
budget cuts and the elimination of several positions. Furthermore, the Bank states that the process
involved the competitive remapping of five operational and technical staff to regional roles and
the departure of eleven staff members from the Water GP across various grade levels.
145. With respect to the legal basis for the Applicant’s redundancy, the Bank contends that
management’s decision was driven by the realignment exercise to adapt to a new organizational
structure. The Bank contends that senior management determined that less emphasis would be
placed on general expertise, particularly in operational support and portfolio management, and that
the shift in skill requirements within the Sustainable Development Vice Presidency was to be
achieved through a combination of batch rotations, recruitment of external candidates, and the exit
of existing staff whose skills no longer aligned with the evolving needs of the organization. The
Bank contends that, after a careful review, management determined that the Applicant’s position,
as well as several other positions, did not align with the new staffing structure, and subsequently
declared a number of positions redundant.
146. Next, the Bank contends that management followed a fair and proper process and made
the FY22 non-shortlisting decision in good faith.
147. With regard to fair and proper processes, the Bank avers that, in addition to being based on
legitimate business needs, the redundancy decision followed all proper procedures in Staff Rule
7.01, paragraph 9.

33
148. First, the Bank contends that the redundancy decision was made by the appropriate
decision-maker, the Vice President, Sustainable Development, in accordance with Staff Rule 7.01,
paragraph 9.03.
149. Second, the Bank contends that the Notice of Redundancy was issued six months prior to
the Applicant’s end of contract and that the redundancy rationale was sufficiently transparent to
the Applicant. The Bank asserts that the Applicant received the Notice of Redundancy on 11 May
2022, which stated that the Applicant would be placed on administrative leave for a six-month
period beginning on 1 July 2022, when the Notice of Redundancy was to take effect. The Bank
asserts that, on 16 May 2022, the Applicant went on a second STD which delayed the
implementation of the redundancy decision for two additional years. The Bank further asserts that
the Applicant returned to work on 16 May 2024, was then placed on six months’ administrative
leave, and was exited from the Bank on 16 November 2024.
150. Third, the Bank contends that it provided the required assistance for alternative
employment to the Applicant. The Bank contends that, since the Applicant was first informed of
the redundancy in July 2021, he has availed himself of the Bank’s career counseling services to
enhance his interview skills. The Bank contends that it also provided retraining opportunities for
the Applicant, including French language classes. In addition to these efforts, the Bank contends
that the Applicant was provided with cross-support opportunities to collaborate on operations in
the Africa Region and analytical assignments to maintain visibility within the organization,
develop critical skills, network, and potentially find other options. The Bank further contends that
Water GP management actively encouraged the Applicant to apply for multiple positions. For
example, the Bank asserts that, after the Applicant had missed the application deadline during the
FY21 batch rotation exercise, the former Water Global Director personally reached out to the
Applicant, on 13 February 2021, to offer him the option to submit his applications directly to HR
to ensure that they would be uploaded into the system.
151. Fourth, the Bank contends that it has complied with its obligation in relation to severance
payments. Specifically, the Bank contends that the Applicant has already accepted the severance
package and is not entitled to reinstatement. The Bank contends that the Applicant’s request for

34
reappointment—whether to a guaranteed position, a mutually agreeable position, or priority
placement in a recruitment exercise—contradicts the explicit terms of the severance package he
accepted. Additionally, the Bank asserts that it understands that the Applicant is now undertaking
a term contract with another international organization, rendering reinstatement impossible.
152. Furthermore, the Bank submits that the Applicant’s claims for reimbursement related to
medical expenses are barred by the exclusive remedy doctrine. The Bank states that, despite having
been compensated for his injury related to the Romania mission, the Applicant now seeks further
compensation for related matters, including reimbursement for medical evaluation costs, lost
wages due to extended STD, and disability insurance coverage. The Bank asserts that the ARP
deemed the Applicant’s Achilles injury compensable on 10 November 2023 and that, consequently,
the Applicant’s legitimate medical expenses were covered retroactively to the date of injury.
153. The Bank contends that the Exclusive Remedy provision under Staff Rule 6.11
unambiguously precludes any further claims arising from the same underlying incident. By
accepting and receiving workers’ compensation benefits, the Bank asserts that the Applicant has
exhausted his legal avenue for redress concerning the Romania mission.
154. Additionally, the Bank avers that the Applicant’s request for reimbursement of self-
evacuation costs lacks merit. The Bank asserts that, at the time of his evacuation, the Applicant
was not stationed in Russia as part of his duty assignment but had chosen to telecommute from
Russia to be with his wife and family. As such, the Bank contends that his decision to evacuate
was a personal matter unrelated to his official duties.
155. Next, the Bank contends that, with respect to the redundancy decision, the Applicant was
afforded all due process obligations and that there were no procedural irregularities.
156. The Bank contends that the Applicant’s assertion that the Bank failed to meaningfully
honor his reentry guarantee to a “suitable position” after his Bishkek field assignment is without
merit. The Bank asserts that the Applicant conveniently omits a critical condition attached to the
reentry guarantee—namely, that the terms of the reentry were subject to change if the field

35
assignment was extended. The Bank further asserts that the Applicant unilaterally requested
multiple extensions of his field assignment—first until March 2019 and then further to 1 November
2019—both of which deviated from standard procedures and were ultimately granted on an
exceptional basis. The Bank contends that Section 8 of the Memorandum of Extended Assignment
to Bishkek governs the present circumstances. Critically, the Bank contends that the Applicant, by
making multiple extension requests, effectively nullified any preexisting obligation the Bank
might have had regarding his reentry under the original terms. To the Bank, Section 8 explicitly
reserves the Bank’s right to modify the reentry conditions upon an extension of the field
assignment, which is precisely what occurred in this case.
157. The Bank contends that the Applicant failed to relocate to Washington, D.C. within the
expected time frame, despite repeated urgings from Water GP management. The Bank avers that
it was only after being informed that his job was at risk due to the Bank-wide realignment exercise
and its impact on the Water GP’s strategic staffing and budgetary constraints that the Applicant
began actively seeking relocation back to Washington, D.C. However, the Bank asserts that, given
that all staff were telecommuting due to the COVID-19 pandemic and international travel
restrictions were in place until mid-2022, physical relocation to Washington, D.C. was neither
practical nor permissible at that time.
158. The Bank contends that the Applicant was also fully informed of his options concerning
exit modalities and financial implications related to redundancy. The Bank asserts that the
Applicant had access to extensive resources outlining the different avenues for departure and their
financial ramifications, supplemented by specific guidance from his HRBP.
159. Finally, the Bank refutes the Applicant’s contentions that the Water GP management acted
outside of the Staff Rules by designating his position as “temporary” and that he was entitled to a
“regular job” upon his return from STD around October 2020. The Bank contends that Tribunal
precedent references a staff member’s “return to work” in the context of “appropriate work
accommodations,” reinforcing that the benchmark for assessing a return to regular duties is the
individual’s own position. The Bank submits that this is precisely what management facilitated for

36
the Applicant, ensuring that he retained his title, salary, and benefits—consistent with any regular
staff member—and fully integrated into the workforce.
The Applicant’s Contention No. 2
The FY22 non-shortlisting decision constituted unfair treatment deserving of significant
compensation
160. The Applicant contends that, with respect to the FY22 batch rotation exercise, Water GP
management failed to adhere to the established recruitment rules. Specifically, the Applicant
contends that he was entitled to be shortlisted for at least one position and that he was unfairly
evaluated against the advertised selection criteria for the Yaoundé position. The Applicant further

The Bank’s Response
The FY22 non-shortlisting decision had reasonable and observable bases, followed fair and
proper processes, and was made in good faith
161. The Bank contends that the non-shortlisting decision for the FY22 batch rotation exercise
had reasonable and observable bases. Specifically, the Bank contends that, with respect to the
FY22 non-shortlisting decision, it has documented its decision-making process, ensuring
transparency and compliance with procedural standards.
162. The Bank asserts that, according to HR guidelines for shortlisting and the broader mobility
framework, the Talent Board has significant discretion in determining whether a candidate’s skills
align with a position’s requirements and whether to shortlist a candidate. The Bank further asserts
that HR and Water GP management testified to PRS that the Applicant was not shortlisted due to
his failure to meet the technical requirements specified for the roles he applied for. The Bank
submits, “Notably, the non-shortlisting decisions were not based on the fact that [the] Applicant
held a temporary position, as is [the] Applicant’s contention.”

37
163. The Bank notes that the PRS Panel found that the decisions not to shortlist the Applicant
for the Antananarivo and Bamako positions were reasonable and had an observable basis, citing
that he lacked key requirements, including strong technical skills, FCV experience, and French
fluency. The Bank further notes, however, that the PRS Panel identified an inconsistency regarding
the Yaoundé position, as the stated reasons for not shortlisting the Applicant—lack of French
fluency and FCV experience—were not listed in the TOR.
164. Next, the Bank contends that management followed fair and proper processes and made
the FY22 non-shortlisting decision in good faith.
165. The Bank states that it disagrees with the conclusions of PRS regarding the FY22 non-
shortlisting decision. The Bank contends that the former Water Global Director’s 7 December 2021
email unequivocally clarified that only Internationally Recruited Staff who had completed four

to the Global Unit in November 2019 and spent a year on STD, did not meet this criterion. As

shortlisted. The Bank further asserts that management’s encouragement for the Applicant to apply
was consistent with its broader efforts to support his career development, as it had done previously.
166. The Bank contends that merely receiving the former Water Global Director’s 7 December
2021 email, which was shared with the entire Water GP, did not automatically confer eligibility to
participate in the recruitment process, let alone for automatic shortlisting. The Bank avers that a
plain reading of the email clearly outlines the criteria, and a seasoned staff member should have

were made by hiring managers in accordance with the Guide to Best Practices in Recruitment and
Selection for Non-Managerial Positions before being reviewed and cleared by the Talent Board.
Further, the Bank contends that detailed and contemporaneous documentation exists to
demonstrate how all candidates, including the Applicant, were evaluated against the selection
criteria.

38
167. Finally, the Bank avers that the record makes clear that all shortlisting decisions and
position selections were made on the basis of merit. The Bank asserts that, while the PRS Panel
scrutinized every aspect of management’s actions in the FY22 batch rotation exercise process and
